baikampady-mangalore-microgrid.summary: SELCO India commissioned a small direct-current solar microgrid in April 2014 for a 75-home informal labor colony in the Baikampady industrial estate north of Mangalore. The documented design combined 1.2 kWp of PV, a nominal 19.2 kWh battery bank, grid-assisted charging, and 96 VDC distribution for efficient lighting, phone charging, three fans, and one television. Canara Bank financed the system through a loan to the property owner, who collected service payments with rent. Public evidence located in this review documents implementation through 2015 but not the system's later operating condition.
baikampady-mangalore-microgrid.status: Commissioned in April 2014 and documented as implemented in a 2015 SELCO site summary. No reliable independent post-2015 operating or decommissioning evidence was found through July 2026, so present operating status is unknown.

organizations.name: SELCO India
organizations.role: Designer and implementer of the solar microgrid; its site documentation also identifies the component configuration and service model.

organizations.name: Canara Bank
organizations.role: Local lender that financed the system through a loan to the colony's property owner.

organizations.name: EmmVee
organizations.role: PV module manufacturer identified in SELCO's documented equipment schedule.

organizations.name: Primetech
organizations.role: Battery manufacturer identified in SELCO's documented equipment schedule.

organizations.name: Power One
organizations.role: Supplier of the custom solar/grid 96 VDC charging unit identified in SELCO's site summary.

specifications.label: PV capacity
specifications.value: 1.2 kWp, configured as four 300 W, 24 V modules in series

specifications.label: Battery bank
specifications.value: 19.2 kWh nominal at 96 VDC: eight 12 V, 200 Ah batteries in series

specifications.label: Customers and connected appliances
specifications.value: 75 households; documented aggregate service was 90 LED lights, 75 phone chargers, three fans, and one television

specifications.label: Household service tiers
specifications.value: 60 homes received one 3 W LED tube and charging point; 15 homes received two 3 W LED tubes plus provision for a television/set-top box or fan

specifications.label: Documented customer charge
specifications.value: INR 140-200 per month, collected with rent to cover the bank loan payment and service costs

equipment.detail: Four 300 W, 24 V modules connected in series for a 1.2 kWp array.
Eight 12 V, 200 Ah batteries in series, yielding a nominal 96 V, 19.2 kWh bank.
Custom solar-and-grid 96 VDC charger with solar-battery-grid source priority.
96 VDC aluminum distribution conductors in PVC conduit, copper service drops, and fuse protection; loads were switched by a manual central timer.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid used 96 VDC distribution and prioritized solar, then battery, then grid-assisted charging. This avoided household AC inverters for the small DC loads.

Because a conventional grid extension to the informal settlement was impractical, the property owner borrowed from Canara Bank, owned the shared plant, and recovered the monthly payment through rent/service collections, displacing kerosene lighting.
SELCO documented a local data logger but no remote monitoring connection.

Searches using the colony, industrial-estate, component, and developer aliases found no reliable operating update after the 2015 SELCO documentation. Current status should not be inferred from undated catalog reproductions.

sources.title: A labour colony gets solar power
Micro-Grid Site Summaries

banggi-island.summary: Sabah Electricity's Banggi Island station is a solar-battery-diesel hybrid supplying homes, schools, and businesses on the island. Current owner documentation gives 1,200 kWp of PV, a 720-cell gel battery bank, and eight diesel generators ranging from 200 kW to 1,000 kW against an approximately 950 kW served load. A Leonics engineering presentation for the project records a 1,200 kWp array, 2,075 kW of inverter capacity, a 2,880 kWh main battery bank, and 1,650 kVA of diesel capacity. The station has received battery, inverter, and diesel upgrades and remains in service, but no source found provides a current annual renewable-energy percentage.
banggi-island.status: Operating under Sabah Electricity as of the owner's current ESG account reviewed in July 2026. That account says battery and inverter upgrades and added diesel capacity have sustained performance. Commissioning dates conflict by phase/source: TNB describes commissioning in 2013, while Leonics records commercial operation on 25 January 2014 and operation since February 2014.

organizations.name: Sabah Electricity Sdn Bhd (SESB)
organizations.role: Owner and operator of the Banggi solar-hybrid station and the island distribution service.

organizations.name: Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB)
organizations.role: Parent utility of SESB and publisher of the current operating account.

organizations.name: Leonics
organizations.role: Supplier of bidirectional battery inverters, PV grid inverters, MPPT equipment, and hybrid control equipment documented for the 1.2 MWp project.

organizations.name: Usaha Sirimas Sdn Bhd
organizations.role: Main contractor identified in Leonics' project record.

specifications.label: Leonics project battery rating
specifications.value: 2,880 kWh main bank; the engineering diagram also shows a 720 kWh remote-bank value, which should not be silently added without phase clarification

specifications.label: Current owner battery description
specifications.value: 720 gel cells; a current energy capacity is not disclosed

specifications.label: Leonics inverter capacity
specifications.value: 2,075 kW total: 1,100 kW battery-side and 975 kW PV/grid-side in the vendor project table

specifications.label: Leonics diesel capacity
specifications.value: 1,650 kVA in the project summary; individual-generator values vary between vendor diagrams and appear to reflect configuration or phase changes

specifications.label: Current diesel fleet
specifications.value: Eight generators, each rated between 200 kW and 1,000 kW; aggregate current capacity is not stated

specifications.label: Served load
specifications.value: Approximately 950 kW serving homes, schools, and businesses

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Solar generation with battery storage and diesel backup/supplement; no verified annual percentage split was located

equipment.detail: The engineering diagram identifies three 300 kW bidirectional inverter blocks (900 kW shown at this level), within a project table that reports 1,100 kW of battery-inverter capacity.
Three 250 kW PV grid-inverter blocks (750 kW) are shown in the main-station diagram.
Three 70 kW MPPT blocks (210 kW total) are shown for DC-coupled PV, alongside three 75 kW remote grid-inverter blocks (225 kW).
Hybrid Control and Communication Unit (HCCU) coordinating PV, batteries, and diesel generation.

technicalDetails.detail: The vendor diagrams show a mixed AC/DC-coupled hybrid: PV grid inverters feed the AC bus, MPPT units charge a 480 VDC battery system, bidirectional inverters form/support the island grid, and diesel generators provide backup and firm capacity.

TNB's current account says the plant was commissioned in 2013. Leonics' project documentation instead gives 25 January 2014 as COD and describes operation since February 2014; these may reflect construction, handover, or phased commercial-operation milestones.

The current owner says battery and inverter upgrades have sustained performance and additional diesel capacity has been installed for high demand. It does not identify the replacement vendors, models, dates, or energy capacity.

A Leonics slide labels 4.93 MW by arithmetically summing PV nameplate, inverter capacity, and diesel capacity. That figure is not a valid independent generating-capacity rating and is not used here as plant output.

sources.title: TNB & SESB: Lighting the Islands
Inverter Technologies in Isolated Microgrid
sources.publisher: Leonics / Asian Development Bank Asia Clean Energy Forum

sources.title: MW Scale Stand-Alone PV-DG Hybrid Systems

bario.summary: The Bario Central Solar Hybrid Power System supplies the remote Bario Highlands with an off-grid combination of PV, battery storage, and diesel backup. Sarawak Energy records construction beginning in 2010, initial commissioning in September 2015, transfer to its operation in June 2016, and an October 2016 official launch. The launch-era rating was 887 kW and at least 70% of generated energy was expected from solar. Vendor documents preserve design changes around 885.84-906 kWp of PV, 3,860 kWh of battery storage, and varying inverter/diesel totals. Sarawak's current Rural Electrification Masterplan describes a 998 kW station serving more than 400 households and proposes a future 33 kV grid interconnection.
bario.status: Operating as an off-grid solar-battery-diesel station under Sarawak Energy in the current Rural Electrification Masterplan reviewed in July 2026. The plan proposes interconnecting Bario to the state grid via an approximately 60 km Ba'kelalan-Bario line and 33 kV substation by 2030; that connection is not yet documented as complete.

organizations.name: Sarawak Energy Berhad
organizations.role: Operator and maintenance authority since the June 2016 handover; its Rural Off-Grid Operation Division employed local operators for the station.

organizations.name: Ministry of Rural and Regional Development (KKLW)
organizations.role: Federal project funder/client; the reported project allocation was RM27 million and KKLW appointed Sarawak Energy for operation and maintenance.

organizations.name: Leonics
organizations.role: Supplier/documenter of the PV, bidirectional inverter, MPPT, and hybrid-control architecture for the Bario system.

specifications.label: Commissioned plant rating (2016 owner account)
specifications.value: 887 kW solar-hybrid system

specifications.label: Current masterplan station rating
specifications.value: 998 kW Bario Central Solar Hybrid Station with three diesel generators

specifications.label: Vendor PV configuration
specifications.value: 885.84 kWp detailed design: 402 kWp AC-coupled plus 483.84 kWp DC-coupled; a later Leonics project table gives 906 kWp, indicating a phase/design revision

specifications.label: Vendor battery rating
specifications.value: 3,860 kWh at 480 VDC

specifications.label: Vendor inverter rating
specifications.value: 1,200 kW in an early project summary; 1,100 kW in a later Leonics table

specifications.label: Vendor diesel rating
specifications.value: 886 kVA in an early project summary; 1,443 kVA in a later Leonics table. An early diagram separately labels 275, 275, 158, and 450 kVA units, consistent with a revised or staged fleet.

specifications.label: Launch-era energy mix
specifications.value: At least 70% solar, with the balance supplied by diesel and battery storage used for balancing/time shifting

specifications.label: Customers
specifications.value: More than 200 households in nine villages plus the bazaar and government facilities at launch; current masterplan says more than 400 households

equipment.detail: Early design diagrams show four 125 kW AC-coupled grid-inverter blocks; the slide's printed aggregate contains an arithmetic inconsistency, so the component count and ratings are retained without adopting its total.
Three 200 kW bidirectional units (600 kW total) connected to the 480 VDC, 3,860 kWh battery bank.
Eight 70 kW MPPT blocks are depicted for the 483.84 kWp DC-coupled PV section. One text extraction renders the multiplier incorrectly; eight units are consistent with the displayed 560 kW total.
Hybrid control and communications coordinate AC-coupled PV, DC-coupled PV/battery, and diesel generation.

technicalDetails.detail: Published engineering analysis describes a mixed-bus system: PV and batteries feed a DC bus through converters while diesel generators feed the AC bus. In the modeled load-following logic, PV serves load and charges storage; the battery discharges to its minimum state of charge before diesel is dispatched.
Construction began in 2010, first commissioning occurred in September 2015, Sarawak Energy accepted operation in June 2016, and the official launch was held on 25 October 2016.

The central plant initially covered nine villages. Two extension villages were expected to lift the central-plant reach above 300 homes, while five other small nearby settlements were assigned separate stand-alone hybrid systems and should not be counted as central-plant capacity.

Sarawak's Rural Electrification Masterplan proposes a 33 kV substation and roughly 60 km covered-conductor link from Ba'kelalan, interconnecting rather than simply replacing the hybrid station under the 2030 plan.

The 887 kW launch rating, 885.84/906 kWp vendor PV values, and 998 kW current masterplan value belong to different dated descriptions. They are retained separately rather than normalized into a single undocumented capacity.

sources.title: Electrification of Bario Highlands via Solar Hybrid Power System
MW Scale Stand-Alone PV-DG Hybrid Systems
Inverter Technologies in Isolated Microgrid

baron-techno-park.summary: Baron Techno Park is an off-grid renewable-energy research and demonstration site at Parang Racuk, Gunungkidul. Its documented hybrid plant combined 36 kW of PV, two wind turbines totaling 15 kW, nominal 25 kW diesel and 25 kW biodiesel generators, a battery bank reported as 288 kVAh/1,200 Ah, and a 25 kVA hybrid controller. The available plant changed over time: a 2017 study said only 24 kW of PV and 5 kW of wind were operating, while a 2023 engineering paper describes a current 36 kW PV/5 kW wind/20 kWh battery/25 kW inverter configuration and reports that the Fortis wind turbine had been stopped by a 2019 malfunction.
baron-techno-park.status: Continues to be documented as an off-grid research/test microgrid. The 5 kW Fortis wind subsystem had been out of service since a 2019 malfunction and was planned for reinstallation in the 2023 paper; no later public confirmation of its return to service was found through July 2026. PV/battery operation is described in 2023, but a recent measured generation mix was not located.

organizations.name: Agency for Assessment and Application of Technology (BPPT)
organizations.role: Original developer and research operator of Baron Techno Park; its functions were later absorbed into Indonesia's BRIN.

organizations.name: Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation (NORAD)
organizations.role: Grant funder associated with the site's establishment in 2009.

organizations.name: Fortis Wind Energy
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the 5 kW wind turbine documented in the 2023 engineering paper.

specifications.label: Installed PV capacity
specifications.value: 36 kW: three arrays, each documented as 120 modules at 120 Wp

specifications.label: Wind capacity by phase
specifications.value: Original installed total 15 kW (10 kW plus 5 kW); the 2023 configuration describes only the 5 kW Fortis unit, which had been stopped since 2019 due to malfunction

specifications.label: Dispatchable generation
specifications.value: 25 kW diesel generator and 25 kW biodiesel generator in the 2017 system inventory

specifications.label: Legacy battery description
specifications.value: 288 kVAh / 1,200 Ah in the 2017 equipment profile; the same paper's introduction says 228 kVAh, an internal conflict

specifications.label: 2023 battery description
specifications.value: 20 kWh

specifications.label: Hybrid inverter/controller
specifications.value: 25 kW/kVA class hybrid inverter and data-acquisition/control system

specifications.label: 2017 study load
specifications.value: 184.7 kWh/day, averaging approximately 7.7 kW

equipment.detail: 5 kW DC-output wind turbine connected through the site's main control room and hybrid inverter; reported stopped since 2019 because of malfunction.

Three nominal 14.4 kWp module groupings are described by component count (120 x 120 Wp each), although the installed project rating is consistently reported as 36 kW; this published arithmetic inconsistency is not silently resolved.
25 kVA-class unit in the main control room, integrating PV, wind, storage, dispatchable generation, and data acquisition.

The 2017 profile describes two arrays, eight strings per array and 15 batteries per string, while also printing 120 cells; the count statements are internally inconsistent. A 2023 paper instead characterizes the active configuration as 20 kWh.

technicalDetails.detail: PV, wind, biodiesel/diesel, battery storage, and inverter resources connect through an off-grid hybrid controller with data acquisition. The site is primarily a demonstration and research platform rather than a utility-scale supply plant.
The 2017 performance study says obstacles left only 24 kW of the 36 kW PV inventory and 5 kW of the 15 kW wind inventory operating at the study time.
The Fortis 5 kW turbine stopped operating in 2019 due to malfunction. The 2023 paper says reinstallation was planned in the near future, but the review found no later commissioning confirmation.
A 2015 site account says the biodiesel demonstration shifted from unsuccessful jatropha feedstock toward used cooking oil; the installation was a pilot/research line, not commercial mass production.
Large annual generation and energy-mix figures in the 2017 paper are HOMER-model results, not metered operating production, and are therefore not presented as an observed energy mix here.

blue-lake-rancheria-microgrid.summary: Blue Lake Rancheria's main community microgrid has operated since 2017 with a 420 kW SolarWorld/SMA PV plant, a 500 kW/950 kWh Tesla Powerpack battery system through two Dynapower inverters, a Siemens Spectrum Power microgrid controller, and a 1 MW Cummins diesel generator. It has islanded through grid outages and supported the surrounding community during disasters. A second Play Station 777 microgrid entered service in 2020, a new Tesla Megapack installation was commissioned in 2024 without a published capacity, and a $5 million 2025 California Energy Commission award is funding a nested 12 kV Microgrid 2.0 expansion.
blue-lake-rancheria-microgrid.status: The 2017 main microgrid and 2020 Play Station 777 microgrid are operational. The tribe reports commissioning an additional Tesla Megapack installation in 2024, but does not disclose its power or energy capacity. The nested Microgrid 2.0 expansion was funded in June 2025 and remained an active demonstration/expansion effort in 2026; it should not yet be counted as commissioned capacity.

organizations.name: Blue Lake Rancheria
organizations.role: Tribal owner, project host, and operator/customer of the community resilience microgrids.

organizations.name: Schatz Energy Research Center
organizations.role: Lead integrator and concept-of-operations developer for the original project; technical partner for the 2025 nested Microgrid 2.0 project.

organizations.name: GHD
organizations.role: Engineer of record, system integrator, construction manager, commissioning agent, civil engineer, and owner's engineer for the original microgrid.

organizations.name: Robert Colburn Electric
organizations.role: Main electrical contractor for the original project outside the solar scope.

organizations.name: REC Solar
organizations.role: Turnkey PV engineering/procurement contractor for the original 420 kW array.

organizations.name: McKeever Energy & Electric
organizations.role: PV installation contractor for the original array.

organizations.name: Tesla
organizations.role: Supplier and integrator of the original ten-Powerpack BESS; the tribe also reports a separate Tesla Megapack installation commissioned in 2024.

organizations.name: Siemens
organizations.role: Supplier of the Spectrum Power microgrid management system and power-flow controls.

organizations.name: Johnson Controls
organizations.role: Supplier/integrator of the Metasys facility energy-management and load-shedding system.

organizations.name: Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E)
organizations.role: Interconnecting utility for the 12.5 kV service and invited utility participant in the nested 12 kV testing program.

organizations.name: California Energy Commission
organizations.role: Public funder and oversight agency for the original EPIC demonstration and the $5 million 2025 nested-microgrid award.

specifications.label: Main microgrid PV
specifications.value: 420 kW, 1,548 SolarWorld 325 W modules through fourteen 30 kW SMA inverters

specifications.label: Main microgrid battery
specifications.value: 500 kW / 950 kWh, ten Tesla Powerpacks through two 250 kW Dynapower inverters

specifications.label: Backup generator
specifications.value: 1 MW Cummins diesel generator

specifications.label: Utility interconnection
specifications.value: 12.5 kV PG&E service through a computer-controlled point-of-common-coupling breaker

specifications.label: Play Station 777 microgrid
specifications.value: 60 kW PV and a battery reported as 169 kWh by the tribe and 174 kWh by the CEC; operational in 2020

specifications.label: 2024 Megapack expansion
specifications.value: New Tesla Megapack installation commissioned in 2024; power and energy ratings were not publicly disclosed in the located source

specifications.label: 2017 renewable contribution
specifications.value: PV supplied approximately 15% of on-site electric load in the first operating year; grid electricity, stored energy, and diesel served the balance according to operating mode

equipment.detail: 1,548 modules forming the 420 kW array.
Fourteen 30 kW units for the original PV plant.
Ten Powerpacks, totaling 500 kW/950 kWh in the original commissioned system.
Two 250 kW power-conversion systems.
Supervisory microgrid management, dispatch, islanding, and power-flow control.
Building-load monitoring and automated load shedding coordinated with the microgrid controller.
Protection and interconnection relay at the utility interface.
1 MW diesel generator for prolonged island operation and resilience.

technicalDetails.detail: The original project finished on schedule with less than 1.5% cost overrun. It saved approximately $160,000 in 2017 and was expected to save about $200,000 annually thereafter while avoiding 159 metric tons CO2e in 2017 (about 175 tons expected annually).

The main system islanded seamlessly during a 2017 outage. Both tribal microgrids remained operational during a three-day 2019 utility outage, and the tribe reports supporting thousands of people after the December 2022 magnitude-6.4 earthquake.

A $5 million CEC award announced 11 June 2025 funds advanced 12 kV nested controls/protection, circuit reconfiguration, and added solar/storage across three facility microgrids, with PG&E invited to testing. Equipment vendors and final capacities were not fixed in the public scope and are not assigned here.
The nested project is intended to inform replication along the approximately 142-mile Hoopa 1101 feeder serving Hoopa Valley, Yurok, and Karuk communities.

The CEC final report for the commissioned 2017 system identifies PV, BESS, grid, and a diesel generator. It does not include the 175 kW biomass/fuel-cell concept found in older planning material; that concept should not be counted as installed main-microgrid capacity.
The tribe hosted an operating-microgrid tour in April 2026 and described ongoing TERAS and nested-microgrid expansion work, providing current evidence that the resilience program remains active.

bonaire-microgrid.summary: Bonaire's island power system is an operating hybrid of 11.1 MW of wind, 5.3 MW of solar, 28.5 MW of thermal generation, and a battery fleet currently described by ContourGlobal as 14 MW. The original 2010 renewable build used twelve Enercon E-44/900 turbines, one E-33/330 turbine, MAN diesel engines, and a 3 MW Saft nickel-cadmium battery. Wärtsilä added a 6 MW/6 MWh battery and GEMS controls in 2019, and ContourGlobal added a further battery and the 5.3 MW solar plant in 2023. The system remains the island's sole bulk supply to distributor WEB, although 2024 demand growth, extreme heat, and cable damage caused outages and prompted capacity and grid upgrades.
bonaire-microgrid.status: Operating under ContourGlobal, with WEB distributing electricity. Current owner figures are 28.5 MW thermal, 11.1 MW wind, 14 MW BESS power, and 5.3 MW solar. WEB reported 2024 reliability problems and added 5 MW of production capacity while upgrading controls and transmission. Wind repowering/expansion is planned, but the publicly stated 60% renewable target was not verified as achieved and no current audited annual energy mix was found.

organizations.name: ContourGlobal Bonaire
organizations.role: Current owner/operator and sole bulk electricity generator supplying WEB Bonaire.

organizations.name: Water- en Energiebedrijf Bonaire (WEB)
organizations.role: Island electricity distributor, grid operator/customer of ContourGlobal generation, and planner of current reliability and energy-transition works.

organizations.name: Evelop / Eneco
organizations.role: Original majority developer, financier, and operator of the 2010 hybrid power plant (reported 90% project interest at development).

organizations.name: Enercon
organizations.role: Original wind-turbine, renewable-system, and load-balancing supplier; held a minority project interest at development.

organizations.name: MAN
organizations.role: Original diesel-genset supplier and minority project participant.

organizations.name: Saft
organizations.role: Supplier of the original 3 MW nickel-cadmium energy-storage system commissioned with the 2010 plant.

organizations.name: Wärtsilä
organizations.role: EPC and controls/storage supplier for the 6 MW/6 MWh 2019 battery expansion and GEMS energy-management system.

organizations.name: SMA Altenso
organizations.role: Engineering and main-component supplier identified by ContourGlobal for the 2023 solar expansion.

organizations.name: Green World Company Projects
organizations.role: Construction contractor identified by ContourGlobal for the 2023 solar project.

specifications.label: Current solar capacity
specifications.value: 5.3 MW, commercially operating from 2023

specifications.label: Current BESS power
specifications.value: 14 MW aggregate power rating; ContourGlobal does not disclose aggregate energy capacity on its current asset page

specifications.label: Current net peak capacity
specifications.value: 58.9 MW as reported by ContourGlobal

specifications.label: Original wind fleet
specifications.value: Twelve 900 kW Enercon E-44 turbines at Morotin (10.8 MW) plus one 330 kW Enercon E-33 at Sorobon, approximately 11.13 MW total

specifications.label: 2019 battery expansion
specifications.value: 6 MW / 6 MWh

specifications.label: Renewable-energy share
specifications.value: WEB describes wind as roughly 22% of annual energy on its current public page and had targeted 60% renewable energy; the review found no audited evidence that the 60% target was achieved after the 2023 solar/BESS additions

equipment.detail: Twelve 900 kW units at Morotin, totaling 10.8 MW.
One 330 kW unit at Sorobon.
3 MW system in three containers at 640 V and 1,320 Ah; designed for more than two minutes of immediate support while diesel generation starts and to absorb power while held near 90% state of charge.
6 MW/6 MWh BESS delivered under EPC scope.
Controls the hybrid thermal, wind, and storage fleet and supports fast frequency response and renewable integration.
30 kV medium-voltage equipment identified in the Dutch government project audit.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind and solar are dispatched with battery storage and thermal generators on a single island grid. The batteries provide fast frequency/power-quality support, absorb excess renewable output, and bridge the interval while diesel units start.

Wärtsilä reports that the 6 MW/6 MWh addition reduced CO2 emissions by 8%, fossil-fuel use by 5%, eliminated renewable curtailment, and increased annual wind utilization by 6-8%. Load-rejection tests demonstrated continued operation after loss of wind, engines, or load without customer shedding.

ContourGlobal's current report describes the additional battery as commercially operating in April 2023 and the 5.3 MW PV plant later in 2023. Local reporting places battery commissioning in July and solar commissioning in September; the difference likely reflects commercial-operation versus local handover milestones.

WEB attributed recent outages to an unexpected 15-20% demand increase, extreme heat, and cable damage. It reported adding 5 MW of production capacity, digitizing controls, and advancing transmission upgrades with a January 2025 target.

ContourGlobal says it is pursuing wind repowering that would more than double wind capacity. WEB also identifies wind-farm expansion in its 2024-2030 transition planning; no completed repower rating was located.

The current 14 MW BESS figure is a power rating. It must not be converted to 14 MWh or simply added to historical energy ratings because the owner does not publish the current aggregate duration/energy capacity.

bornholm-island.summary: Bornholm is a live island power system and long-running smart-grid test platform connected to Sweden by one subsea cable. EcoGrid EU enrolled roughly 2,000 households in 2011-2015, and EcoGrid 2.0 concluded in 2019 after remotely coordinating electric heating in 800 homes. Under normal conditions BEOF says its own local generation is entirely wind, solar, biomass, and biogas, while the island imports Nordic electricity when local renewable output is insufficient and starts Rønne thermal units during cable outages. BEOF announced a 10 MW Hitachi Energy battery for installation in the old Rønne coal plant in 2026, but no public commissioning confirmation was found by this review.
bornholm-island.status: EcoGrid EU and EcoGrid 2.0 are complete demonstrations; Bornholm remains an operating island grid and active Test Island Bornholm platform. BEOF and Energinet signed a May 2026 reserve-supply agreement for 2027-2031. A 10 MW Hitachi Energy battery was announced for 2026 installation but is not counted as commissioned absent confirmation. The separate 3 GW Bornholm Energy Island offshore project was revived by a January 2026 Danish-German agreement and remains future infrastructure.

organizations.name: Bornholms Energi & Forsyning (BEOF)
organizations.role: Local electric-grid owner/operator, generation owner, Test Island host, EcoGrid partner, and party responsible for reserve island supply.

organizations.name: Technical University of Denmark (DTU)
organizations.role: Research lead/partner for EcoGrid demonstrations and operator of experimental monitoring, forecasting, and SCADA research infrastructure.

organizations.name: Danish Energy Association
organizations.role: EcoGrid 2.0 consortium participant and project coordinator in the completed 2016-2019 demonstration.

organizations.name: IBM
organizations.role: EcoGrid 2.0 technology/consortium participant.

organizations.name: Energinet
organizations.role: Danish transmission-system operator and counterparty to BEOF's 2027-2031 reserve-supply agreement for Bornholm.

organizations.name: Hitachi Energy
organizations.role: Supplier selected for BEOF's announced 10 MW indoor battery at the former Rønne coal plant.

specifications.label: Island electricity demand
specifications.value: Approximately 250 GWh/year, with a roughly 55 MW peak and about 28,000 electricity customers in DTU's technical system description

specifications.label: Local renewable share
specifications.value: BEOF says local production rose from about 50% to roughly 75% of annual island consumption over the prior decade; a separate BEOF page says its wind and solar average about 65% of consumption daily. These are different measures and not interchangeable.

specifications.label: Normal BEOF production mix
specifications.value: BEOF characterizes 100% of its own local electricity production under normal conditions as wind, solar, biomass, and biogas; this does not mean all island consumption is renewable because Nordic grid imports cover shortfalls.

specifications.label: DTU documented generation fleet
specifications.value: 35 wind turbines totaling 30 MW, 5 MW PV, 2 MW biogas engines, 34 MW across 14 oil diesels, a 25 MW oil steam unit, and a 37 MW multi-fuel oil/coal/wood-chip steam unit; the page was updated in 2022 and its fleet figures should be treated as dated

specifications.label: Announced battery
specifications.value: 10 MW indoor BESS, approximately 100 tonnes, planned in the old Rønne coal plant during 2026; MWh/duration not disclosed

specifications.label: EcoGrid participation
specifications.value: About 2,000 households in EcoGrid EU (2011-2015); 800 households with remotely controlled heat pumps/electric heaters in the completed EcoGrid 2.0 field trial

specifications.label: Future Bornholm Energy Island
specifications.value: 3 GW offshore wind connection linking Danish and German systems, intended to supply electricity equivalent to about three million homes; not current Bornholm microgrid capacity

equipment.detail: Operational at BEOF with an experimental counterpart at DTU; approximately one-second system signals support research and operation.
Ten grid recorders plus seven plant-level recorders are described in DTU's research instrumentation inventory.
Five-minute customer smart-meter data, PMUs, and wind/PV/load forecasting feed the island research platform.
10 MW indoor BESS intended for grid balancing, ancillary services, and faster black start; energy capacity was not published.

technicalDetails.detail: Bornholm is connected to the Swedish/Nordic grid by a single subsea cable. When that connection is unavailable, BEOF maintains island-operation capability using local reserve plant.

The 2016-2019 demonstration remotely coordinated heat pumps and electric heaters in 800 homes to test flexible demand against grid and market needs. The earlier target of 1,000 devices/households should not replace the realized 800-household figure.
BEOF reports more than ten years as a full-scale test island, a portfolio valued around DKK650 million, and more than EUR100 million of project activity handled.

The May 2026 BEOF-Energinet agreement requires BEOF to maintain reserve generation and response for 2027-2031 when the Sweden cable is unavailable, replacing the arrangement that runs through the end of 2026.
BEOF announced the 10 MW battery on 13 March 2025 for installation during 2026. No public commissioning confirmation was found by 20 July 2026, so it remains classified as announced/in installation.

The January 2026 Danish-German agreement revived the separate 3 GW Bornholm Energy Island offshore transmission project. It is future interconnector/offshore-wind infrastructure and must not be added to the current island microgrid fleet.

borrego-microgrid.summary: SDG&E's Borrego Springs community microgrid serves an isolated desert feeder with extensive local solar, batteries, an ultracapacitor, diesel backup, and advanced islanding controls. The original system supported thousands of customers during major 2013 outages. A major Microgrid 3.0 upgrade replaces two 1.8 MW diesels with two 2.5 MW Tier IV Final units and adds six Tesla battery containers totaling 7 MW/14 MWh. Replacement generators were commissioned in March 2025, while June 2025 community minutes say the battery was still commissioning and local/remote control integration remained unfinished. DOE/NREL work nevertheless completed field validation of renewable-led black start and islanding using a grid-forming battery and PXiSE controller.
borrego-microgrid.status: Operating community microgrid in a major upgrade/integration phase. The two 2.5 MW replacement diesels were commissioned and functional by March 2025. The 7 MW/14 MWh Tesla BESS was still commissioning on 4 June 2025, with local controls targeted for September and remote integration by year-end; no later official completion confirmation was found through July 2026. A proposed 250 kW, 16-hour hydrogen resource also remains unverified as commissioned.

organizations.name: SDG&E
organizations.role: Utility owner, operator, lead developer, controls integrator, and interconnection authority for the Borrego Springs community microgrid.

organizations.name: California Energy Commission
organizations.role: Public funder and research partner for the original community microgrid and subsequent lessons/upgrade work.

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy
organizations.role: Funder of the original Renewable and Distributed Systems Integration demonstration and the later Solar Energy Technologies Office renewable-black-start project.

organizations.name: National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL)
organizations.role: Research and validation partner for controller-in-the-loop, grid-forming BESS, renewable black-start, and islanding tests.

organizations.name: PXiSE Energy Solutions
organizations.role: Supplier of the later microgrid controller used in the renewable-led black-start and grid-forming-storage work.

organizations.name: Tesla
organizations.role: Supplier identified in community project updates for the six-container, 7 MW/14 MWh upgrade battery system.

organizations.name: Saft
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the original 500 kW/1.5 MWh battery system.

organizations.name: Parker Hannifin
organizations.role: Integrator/power-conversion partner for the original 500 kW/1.5 MWh Saft battery.

organizations.name: IBM
organizations.role: Original RDSI project-management participant.

organizations.name: Horizon Energy
organizations.role: Original architecture, business-process, and systems-integration participant (identified more fully as Horizon Energy Group in the project review).

organizations.name: Motorola
organizations.role: Original network communications and security participant.

organizations.name: Oracle
organizations.role: Original outage-management and distribution-management systems participant.

organizations.name: Lockheed Martin
organizations.role: Original RDSI consortium participant; the located sources do not assign a narrower equipment role.

organizations.name: University of San Diego
organizations.role: Original project participant supporting regulatory-design work.

organizations.name: UCSD
organizations.role: Legacy catalog attribution not corroborated by the original DOE partner slide; the named academic partner was University of San Diego, not University of California San Diego.

organizations.name: AES
organizations.role: Legacy attribution requiring disambiguation: later technical reports use AES 1, AES 2, and AES 3 as asset identifiers for energy-storage systems, not as evidence that AES Corporation supplied or owned them.

organizations.name: Anza-Borrego Desert Natural History Association
organizations.role: Community partner for the current upgrade and resilience work.

organizations.name: Borrego Water District
organizations.role: Community/critical-services partner for the current upgrade.

specifications.label: Community served
specifications.value: Approximately 2,500 residential and 300 commercial customers on a single radial feeder

specifications.label: Local solar resource
specifications.value: Approximately 26 MW utility-scale PV plus roughly 3 MW customer rooftop PV in the CEC final report; the CPUC later describes the microgrid as supporting about 40 MW of local solar resources

specifications.label: Legacy battery fleet
specifications.value: 1.5 MW / 4.5 MWh total across AES 1 (0.5 MW/1.5 MWh) and AES 2 (1 MW/3 MWh), equivalent to three hours at aggregate nameplate power

specifications.label: Ultracapacitor
specifications.value: Approximately 0.25-0.3 MW with about 30 seconds of support, depending on rounded source description

specifications.label: Legacy diesel generation
specifications.value: Two 1.8 MW units, 3.6 MW total

specifications.label: Replacement diesel generation
specifications.value: Two 2.5 MW Tier IV Final units, 5 MW total; commissioned by March 2025

specifications.label: Microgrid 3.0 battery
specifications.value: Six containers totaling 7 MW / 14 MWh (two-hour duration); still commissioning in June 2025

specifications.label: Planned hydrogen resource
specifications.value: 250 kW with 16-hour duration in the CPUC's Microgrid 3.0 plan; no commissioning evidence located

specifications.label: Typical peak load
specifications.value: Approximately 14 MW in the CEC's 2024 lessons report

equipment.detail: 500 kW/1.5 MWh battery manufactured by Saft and integrated with Parker Hannifin power conversion.
The 1 MW/3 MWh AES 2 storage asset was upgraded for grid-forming operation and used as the island-grid leader in renewable black-start testing. 'AES' is an asset label in the technical paper.
Coordinates batteries, ultracapacitor, diesel generation, switching, load management, and renewable-led black start in the later control architecture.
Six battery containers totaling 7 MW/14 MWh; installation began in August 2024 and commissioning was ongoing in June 2025.
Two 2.5 MW Tier IV Final units replacing the original two 1.8 MW machines; commissioned and functional by March 2025. The located sources do not identify the manufacturer/model.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid normally remains grid-connected/dormant while local solar exports. During daytime island operation it can serve the whole community; at night it prioritizes critical loads and can shed noncritical load according to available battery and fuel.
After an April 2013 windstorm the microgrid served 1,225 customers for about six hours. During a September 2013 storm that downed 20 poles, it served 1,060 customers for roughly 25 hours.

CEC's 2024 review records several successful planned outages but also failed islanding demonstrations caused by controller-communications and frequency-stabilization problems, showing that nameplate capacity alone did not guarantee successful transitions.

Community minutes report that both legacy generators failed during a 61-hour planned February 2023 microgrid operation, taking the system out of service. The replacement generators were commissioned by March 2025.

The DOE/NREL project completed modeling, controller/hardware-in-the-loop work, and field tests in which a grid-forming battery led black start and islanding instead of diesel. Synchrophasor and power-quality measurements verified the fast response, and the DERMS interface was replicated at other SDG&E sites.

SDG&E's 2024 fact sheet expected completion in Q2 2025, but 4 June 2025 minutes say batteries were still commissioning, local controls were targeted for September, and remote control-center integration for year-end. The later direct evidence supersedes the forecast.

The CPUC describes planned CAISO market participation for the upgraded assets; it also says the then-current microgrid did not provide resource adequacy. This is a plan, not verified operating revenue/service.
